question_text: How frequently must covered entities re-enroll in OPAIS?

answers.text: Monthly
Quarterly
Annually
Every three years

explanation: Covered entities must re-certify their participation in OPAIS annually. Failure to re-certify results in removal from the program.

question_text: Within how many business days must a covered entity report changes in OPAIS?

answers.text: 5 business days
10 business days
30 calendar days
60 calendar days

explanation: HRSA requires covered entities to update OPAIS within 10 business days of any material changes.

question_text: During an HRSA audit, which finding would most likely result in a covered entity being required to repay manufacturers?

answers.text: Using a contract pharmacy that is more than 40 miles from the covered entity
Evidence of drug diversion — 340B drugs dispensed to non-patients
Failure to use the Prime Vendor Program (PVP)
Purchasing drugs from a secondary wholesaler instead of the Prime Vendor

explanation: Drug diversion is a material violation. When HRSA audits find diversion, covered entities may be required to repay manufacturers the 340B discount received on diverted drugs.

question_text: What is the 340B ceiling price formula?

answers.text: Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) minus the Unit Rebate Amount (URA)
Wholesale Acquisition Cost (WAC) minus 23.1%
Best Price minus the Medicaid rebate percentage
Average Sales Price (ASP) minus 6%

explanation: The 340B ceiling price = AMP - URA, where URA is the Medicaid Unit Rebate Amount.

question_text: Which organization operates the Prime Vendor Program on behalf of HRSA?

answers.text: McKesson Corporation
Apexus
AmerisourceBergen
340B Health

explanation: Apexus serves as the HRSA-contracted Prime Vendor for the 340B program, negotiating sub-ceiling prices and providing compliance resources.

question_text: What must a covered entity do when an HRSA audit finds a credible allegation of a violation?

answers.text: File a lawsuit against HRSA within 30 days
Submit a corrective action plan (CAP) and, if applicable, repay manufacturers for overcharges or misuse
Immediately terminate all contract pharmacy agreements
Request a congressional hearing

explanation: When HRSA finds a violation, the covered entity must submit a corrective action plan. Material violations can require repayment to affected manufacturers.

question_text: Manufacturers are required to make 340B pricing available as a condition of participation in which program?

answers.text: Medicare Part D
Medicaid Drug Rebate Program (MDRP)
340B Prime Vendor Program
Federal Supply Schedule

explanation: Manufacturers must participate in the MDRP and, as a condition, must agree to provide covered outpatient drugs to covered entities at or below the 340B ceiling price.

question_text: When a covered entity believes it has been charged more than the 340B ceiling price, what is the proper initial remedy?

answers.text: File an ADR complaint immediately with HRSA
Contact the manufacturer directly to request a refund or credit
Stop purchasing from that manufacturer entirely
Report to CMS and request a Medicaid audit

explanation: The first step when overcharged is to contact the manufacturer directly. If unresolved, the ADR process is available.

answers.text: The drug's wholesale cost exceeds $100 per unit
The AMP minus URA formula produces a result at or below zero
The covered entity purchases fewer than 100 units
The drug is classified as a specialty pharmaceutical

explanation: Penny pricing occurs when AMP - URA ≤ $0.00, in which case HRSA sets the ceiling price at $0.01.

question_text: Which of the following is a best practice for maintaining 340B audit readiness?

answers.text: Conducting self-audits only when HRSA notifies the entity of an upcoming audit
Maintaining a standing compliance committee, conducting annual self-audits, and keeping TPA reports for at least 3 years
Limiting contract pharmacy agreements to reduce audit scope
Delegating all compliance responsibility to the split-billing software vendor

explanation: Audit readiness requires proactive, ongoing compliance activities: standing compliance committee, regular self-audits, document retention, and annual staff training.
